Knowing this can help clear the ground. When Muslims and Christians talk about the Trinity, they are not always talking about the same thing – and it helps to be clear about what Christians actually claim before engaging the question.

elements.text: Part 1: Jesus and the Father

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>‘Do not let your hearts be distressed. You believe in God; believe also in me. <sup>2</sup>There are many dwelling places in my Father’s house. Otherwise, I would have told you, because I am going away to make ready a place for you. <sup>3</sup>And if I go and make ready a place for you, I will come again and take you to be with me, so that where I am you may be too. <sup>4</sup>And you know the way where I am going.’ <sup>5</sup>Thomas said to him, ‘Lord, we don’t know where you are going. How can we know the way?’ <sup>6</sup>Jesus replied, ‘I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me. <sup>7</sup>If you have known me, you will know my Father too. And from now on you do know him and have seen him.’ <sup>8</sup>Philip said to him, ‘Lord, show us the Father, and we will be content.’ <sup>9</sup>Jesus replied, ‘Have I been with you for so long, and you have not known me, Philip? The person who has seen me has seen the Father! How can you say, “Show us the Father”? <sup>10</sup>Do you not believe that I am in the Father, and the Father is in me? The words that I say to you, I do not speak on my own initiative, but the Father residing in me performs his miraculous deeds. <sup>11</sup>Believe me that I am in the Father, and the Father is in me, but if you do not believe me, believe because of the miraculous deeds themselves.’

elements.question: Philip asks Jesus to show them the Father – and expects that this would be a separate, additional revelation. How does Jesus respond to that expectation, and what does his answer reveal about who he is?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus rejects Philip’s assumption entirely. Philip expected the Father to be somewhere else — a separate figure who could be shown or pointed to. But Jesus says: the person who has seen me has seen the Father. He is not a window onto God or a messenger from God; he is the one in whom the Father is present. He goes on to say that he does not even speak on his own initiative — the Father residing in him performs his deeds. This means Jesus is not claiming to be a very clear picture of God. He is claiming that the Father is actually present and active in him. That is a claim no prophet could make, and it tells us that Jesus understands himself to be more than a representative — he is the place where the Father is encountered directly.
elements.questionHint: Look at verse 9 — when Philip asks Jesus to show them the Father, what exactly does Jesus say he would have expected Philip to already understand from knowing him?

elements.question: Jesus uses the language of mutual indwelling – he is in the Father and the Father is in him. What does this language suggest about their relationship? Is this how you would describe two separate beings?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The language of mutual indwelling — I am in the Father and the Father is in me — is not how you would describe two separate individuals. Two separate persons can be close, can agree, can work together, but one cannot be inside the other in any meaningful sense. Jesus uses this language twice in the passage, suggesting it is not casual or accidental. He is describing a relationship of shared being, not merely shared purpose or agreement. The Father does not simply send Jesus or instruct him from a distance — the Father resides in him and performs deeds through him. This is the language of unity, not cooperation between two distinct and independent beings. It points toward something more profound than any human relationship can fully describe.
elements.questionHint: Try to think of two people who are completely separate from each other — could you ever truthfully say that one is literally inside the other and the other inside the first, or does that kind of language only make sense for things that share the same being?

elements.text: Jesus’ words here are stunning in their directness. He does not say, “I will point you to the Father,” or “I will tell you about the Father.” He says: “The person who has seen me has seen the Father.” This is not a claim that Jesus and the Father are identical – he speaks of the Father as distinct from himself throughout these chapters. But it is a claim that Jesus is the full and final revelation of who God is. To know Jesus is to know God. No messenger or prophet has ever said anything like this – because none could.
Part 2: The Holy Spirit – A Person, Not a Force

elements.passageText: <sup>15</sup>‘If you love me, you will obey my commandments. <sup>16</sup>Then I will ask the Father, and he will give you another Advocate to be with you forever– <sup>17</sup>the Spirit of truth, whom the world cannot accept, because it does not see him or know him. But you know him, because he resides with you and will be in you.’

elements.question: Jesus calls the Spirit “another Advocate” – the Greek word is Paraclete, meaning one who comes alongside to help, defend, and counsel. What does calling the Spirit another Advocate imply about the Spirit’s relationship to Jesus himself?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The word another is crucial. Jesus does not say the Father will send an Advocate, or a helper of a different kind. He says another Advocate — implying that Jesus himself has been the first Advocate, the one who has been alongside the disciples helping, defending, and guiding them. The Spirit is therefore not a replacement of a lesser kind; the Spirit will do for the disciples what Jesus has been doing, but now permanently and from within rather than alongside. This places the Spirit in the same category as Jesus himself — one who comes from the Father, performs the same role, and carries the same authority. The word another quietly establishes a deep equivalence between the Spirit and the Son.
elements.questionHint: Focus on the single word ‘another’ — if the Spirit is another Advocate, what does that word imply Jesus himself has been to the disciples up to this point?

elements.question: Notice that Jesus uses personal pronouns for the Spirit – “he resides with you,” “he will be in you.” Why does it matter that the Spirit is a person and not simply an impersonal power or force?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage

deeper.label: Go deeper…
deeper.question: The Spirit is described as the one who will be in believers – residing within them permanently. How does the presence of the Spirit change the way we relate to God day to day? What difference does it make practically?

elements.sampleAnswer: It matters enormously. An impersonal force or energy cannot know you, cannot guide you, cannot be grieved or responded to in relationship. You can harness an impersonal power, but you cannot know it. Jesus consistently uses personal pronouns — he resides, he will be in you — language that points to someone who is present, aware, and in relationship. The Spirit is also described as the Spirit of truth, implying that the Spirit has knowledge, discernment, and communicates truly. This means that what believers receive is not a spiritual charge or a religious experience. It is the indwelling of a person — God present in the most intimate possible way. That transforms the whole nature of the Christian life from religious duty into personal relationship.
elements.questionHint: Ask yourself what the difference would be between receiving an impersonal power and being indwelt by a person who knows you — what can a person do that a force or energy simply cannot?

elements.text: For many who come from a background where God is understood primarily as transcendent and distant – above and beyond creation – the idea that God’s own Spirit lives within the believer is almost overwhelming. This is not a vague spiritual energy. This is God himself, present and personal, dwelling in those who trust in Jesus. The same Spirit that hovered over the waters at creation, that rested on the prophets, that came upon Mary – this Spirit now makes his home in every person who belongs to Christ.
Part 3: The Glory Shared Before Creation

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>When Jesus had finished saying these things, he looked upward to heaven and said, ‘Father, the time has come. Glorify your Son, so that your Son may glorify you– <sup>2</sup>just as you have given him authority over all humanity, so that he may give eternal life to everyone you have given him. <sup>3</sup>Now this is eternal life: that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom you have sent. <sup>4</sup>I glorified you on earth by completing the work you gave me to do. <sup>5</sup>And now, Father, glorify me at your side with the glory I had with you before the world was created.’

elements.question: Jesus prays for the Father to restore “the glory I had with you before the world was created.” What does this tell us about where Jesus came from and who he was before he was born in Bethlehem?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus is claiming pre-existence — that he existed before the world was created, and that in that prior existence he already shared glory with the Father. This is not the language of a created being awaiting birth. A prophet is born, lives, and dies; their existence is bounded by time. But Jesus speaks of a glory he already had with the Father before creation itself. He is asking to be restored to a state he was in before anything existed. This means Jesus did not begin in Bethlehem. His human birth was an entry into the world, not his beginning. He is describing himself as one who has always been with the Father — sharing in the divine glory — and who temporarily set that aside to complete his mission on earth.
elements.questionHint: Notice that Jesus says he had this glory ‘before the world was created’ — what does it mean about when Jesus began to exist if he already possessed something before creation itself?

elements.question: In verse 3, Jesus defines eternal life as knowing God – and then immediately places himself alongside the Father as the one through whom that knowing happens. What does this say about Jesus’ role in our relationship with God?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus defines eternal life as knowing the only true God and Jesus Christ whom he has sent. The striking thing is the structure: he places himself alongside the Father as equally necessary to that knowing. He does not say eternal life is knowing God, and that Jesus will help with that. He says eternal life is knowing the Father and knowing Jesus — as if the two are inseparable. You cannot have the first without the second. This means Jesus is not a gateway that you pass through and leave behind; he is himself part of what eternal life consists of. Knowing Jesus is not a means to knowing God — it is itself part of knowing God. This places Jesus in a unique position that no prophet, teacher, or messenger occupies.
elements.questionHint: Read verse 3 carefully and notice its exact structure — is Jesus saying that knowing him is a means of reaching God, or that knowing him is itself part of what eternal life is?

elements.text: This prayer is unlike anything recorded in any other religious text. Jesus does not pray as a prophet seeking God’s guidance. He prays as a Son who shared the Father’s glory before time began – and who is now returning to that glory after completing his mission on earth. The intimacy, the equality, the shared glory: none of this can be explained if Jesus is simply a great human messenger. This is why Christians do not simply respect Jesus. They worship him – alongside the Father and the Spirit – because this is what Jesus himself revealed as right and true.
Part 4: One Name – Three Persons

elements.passageText: <sup>19</sup>Therefore go and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit,’

elements.question: In Matthew 28:19, Jesus says to baptise in the name (singular) of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. Why is it significant that Jesus uses one name – not three separate names?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: In the Old Testament and Jewish thought, a name was not just a label — it referred to identity, nature, and authority. To act in someone’s name was to act with their authority and to represent their person. Jesus says to baptise in the name — one name — of the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit. If these were three separate beings, three separate names would be expected. The use of the singular name for all three together is a compressed but clear statement that the three share one identity, one being, one authority. It does not say three names, or in the names of. The grammar itself carries a theological claim: these three are one.
elements.questionHint: Notice the grammar carefully: Jesus says ‘in the name’ — singular — for three persons together. What would you naturally expect him to say if the Father, Son, and Spirit were three completely separate beings?

elements.passageText: <sup>14</sup>The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ and the love of God and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit be with you all.’

elements.question: Paul’s blessing in 2 Corinthians 13:14 was written around AD 55 – within 25 years of Jesus’ death and resurrection. It places Father, Son, and Spirit together naturally, without any explanation or argument. What does this suggest about how early the Trinitarian understanding was in the church?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The fact that Paul uses this Trinitarian structure without explanation or argument is telling. He does not say: as you know, Christians believe in Father, Son, and Spirit — let me explain. He simply uses it as a blessing, as if it is already familiar and settled for his readers. This means the Trinitarian pattern was not the result of long theological development or later church councils. It was already part of how early Christians thought about God within a generation of the resurrection. AD 55 is well within living memory of Jesus. The people Paul was writing to could have spoken with eyewitnesses. The Trinitarian shape of Christian faith is not a later addition — it is present at the very beginning of the written record.
elements.questionHint: Notice that Paul uses this pattern with no introduction or explanation — what does it suggest about how familiar and established this way of speaking about God already was among the people he was writing to?

elements.text: A Simple Framework for the Trinity

The Trinity is a mystery – but it is a revealed mystery, not an invented one. Here are three things that Christians can say clearly:

1. There is one God. Christians are not polytheists. The God of the Old Testament and the God of the New Testament are the same God. He alone is Creator, Lord, and Saviour.

2. Within that one God, there is an eternal relationship. Father, Son, and Holy Spirit are genuinely distinct – the Father is not the Son, the Son is not the Spirit – but they are not three separate gods. They are one in being, one in will, and one in love.

3. We know this because Jesus himself revealed it. The Trinity is not the result of philosophical speculation. It is the result of listening carefully to what Jesus said and did. He spoke of the Father as distinct from himself. He promised to send the Spirit. He shared glory with the Father before creation. The Trinitarian shape of God is shown in Scripture – not imposed upon it.

elements.instruction: Choose the option that best reflects where you are right now – there are no right or wrong answers here.
statements: I feel I have a clearer understanding of what the Trinity means after this chapter.
The idea that God is three persons and yet one God makes sense to me.
I find it helpful that the Trinity is based on what Jesus himself said, rather than on later church teaching.

elements.text: Common Questions

elements.term: “Isn’t the Trinity just shirk – associating partners with God?”
elements.question: This is the most important question in this chapter. What do Christians actually mean when they talk about the Trinity – and what are they not saying?
elements.answer: Shirk – associating created beings or rivals with God – is indeed a grave error, and Christians agree with that completely. But the Trinity is not a claim that God has partners or competitors. It is a claim that the one God eternally exists in a relationship of Father, Son, and Spirit. Jesus is not a second god added to the first; he is the eternal Son within the being of the one God. Christians do not worship a created being alongside God – they worship the God who has revealed himself in this Trinitarian way. The concern behind the question is right and good. The Christian answer is that the Trinity, properly understood, is not shirk – it is the revealed nature of the one God.

elements.term: “Was the Trinity invented at the Council of Nicaea in AD 325?”
elements.question: Many people believe the Trinity was a later addition to Christianity. Is that true?
elements.answer: The Council of Nicaea (AD 325) did not invent the Trinity – it defended it against those who wanted to subtract from it. The council was responding to a teacher called Arius who argued that the Son was a created being, subordinate to the Father. Nicaea said: no – the Son is of the same nature as the Father, just as Scripture teaches. The Trinitarian structure itself is found in the very earliest New Testament documents: 2 Corinthians 13:14 was written around AD 55, less than 25 years after the crucifixion. Jesus himself speaks in Trinitarian patterns throughout John 14-17. Nicaea clarified and defended what was already there. It did not create it.

elements.term: “How can God have a Son? Doesn’t that imply something physical?”
elements.question: The phrase “Son of God” can sound biological or physical. What does it actually mean?
elements.answer:
This is an understandable concern, and it is important to answer it clearly. “Son of God” does not mean that God had a child through any physical process. It is not that kind of language.

Arabic actually has two different words that are relevant and helpful here. Walad refers to a biological offspring – a child born through physical means. Ibn refers to a son in the relational, originating sense – one who comes from another, bears their nature, and stands in close relationship. The Quran’s objection to the idea of God having a son is specifically to the walad – and Christians agree completely: that is not what is meant. The Bible’s claim is of the ibn kind: Jesus is the one who originates from the very being of God, shares his nature, and stands in eternal relationship with the Father.

The Nicene Creed, written in the fourth century to address exactly this question, puts it this way: Jesus is “begotten, not made.” That phrase is worth reflecting on. Begotten means he comes from the Father – he shares the Father’s nature. Not made means he is not a creation, not something that came into existence at a point in time. He has always existed in relationship with the Father. “Son of God” names that relationship – the one who eternally proceeds from God’s own being, not the one born through any physical act.

There is something else worth noticing. The Quran itself calls Jesus kalimatuhu – “his Word” (An-Nisa 4:171). The Bible explains what that means: not a word written in a book, but the living Word who proceeded from God’s very being and entered the world as a person. “Son of God” and “Word of God” are two ways of pointing at the same truth – that Jesus comes from God, shares God’s nature, and is not a created being like any other.

elements.text:
Before we move to reflection, here is one reason why the Trinity is not just a puzzle to wrestle with – but a comfort to rest in.

The Bible says that God is love (1 John 4:8). But love, by its nature, requires someone to love. If God were a single, solitary person before creation existed, he could not have been love – he would have needed the world in order to have someone to love. That would mean God needed us to be fully himself.

The Trinity means something entirely different: God’s love did not begin when he made the world. It was already flowing, from eternity, between Father, Son, and Spirit – a relationship of self-giving love that has always existed. God did not create us because he needed love. He created us because he is love, and love overflows. Love is not an afterthought, relationships are not an afterthought. Relationships are central to who God is, and to all of reality. Love is central to who God is, and to all of reality.

This is also why Jesus could pray in John 17:24: “You loved me before the creation of the world.” The love that reached you is not new. It is eternal – and you have been welcomed into it.
Personal Reflection

The Trinity is not meant to be a puzzle to solve before you can come to God. It is a description of the God who has already come to you – in the person of Jesus, by the presence of the Spirit. You do not need to fully understand a person before you can know them. The same is true of God. Reflection is not doubt – it is the beginning of deeper knowing.

elements.question: What is the most helpful thing you have learned or seen in this chapter about who God is?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: Is there something about the Trinity that still feels difficult or unsettled for you? What question would you most want to sit with this week?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.text: The God revealed in Jesus is not a distant, unknowable force – and he is not a simple, solitary being locked away in transcendence. He is a God of relationship, of love, of eternal self-giving. Before the world existed, Father, Son, and Spirit were already in a communion of love. And the extraordinary news of the gospel is that you have been invited into that communion. Not as an outsider. Not as a servant who must earn entry. But as one whom the Father calls his own, in the Son, by the Spirit. That is the Trinity – not a riddle, but a relationship, opened wide for you.

elements.question:
Christians throughout history have been asked: What do you believe? Who do you think Jesus is? Sometimes those questions come in a quiet conversation. Sometimes they come in a charged or difficult moment, when composing a careful answer is hard. It helps to have words already in your heart.

The Nicene Creed was written in the fourth century specifically to answer the question of who Jesus is in relation to the Father. It has been confessed by Christians across every tradition, in every country, ever since. It is not a magic formula – it is a gift: a clear, ancient answer you can carry with you.

Read it slowly. Notice what it says about the Son.
elements.answer:
We believe in one God, the Father, the Almighty, maker of heaven and earth, of all that is, seen and unseen.

We believe in one Lord, Jesus Christ, the only Son of God, eternally begotten of the Father, God from God, Light from Light, true God from true God, begotten, not made, of one Being with the Father. Through him all things were made. For us and for our salvation he came down from heaven: by the power of the Holy Spirit he became incarnate from the Virgin Mary, and was made man. For our sake he was crucified under Pontius Pilate; he suffered death and was buried. On the third day he rose again in accordance with the Scriptures; he ascended into heaven and is seated at the right hand of the Father. He will come again in glory to judge the living and the dead, and his kingdom will have no end.

We believe in the Holy Spirit, the Lord, the giver of life, who proceeds from the Father. With the Father and the Son he is worshiped and glorified. He has spoken through the Prophets.

We believe in one holy, universal, and apostolic Church. We acknowledge one baptism for the forgiveness of sins. We look for the resurrection of the dead, and the life of the world to come. Amen.

If this is new to you, you do not need to memorise it immediately. But consider returning to it this week – reading it slowly, line by line, as a prayer and a declaration. Christians in your land, and in every land, have been saying these words for seventeen centuries.

elements.question:
This week, read John 14:1-17 again slowly – as if Jesus is speaking directly to you. Notice every time he refers to the Father, to himself, and to the Spirit. As you read, ask: What is God saying to me through these words?

If you pray this week, try beginning with: “Father…” – the word Jesus himself used. Notice how it feels.
Close your time together with this prayer – or use it as a starting point for your own words.
elements.answer: Father, thank you that you have not left us to guess who you are. Thank you for sending your Son to show us your face. Thank you for sending your Spirit to live within us. We do not fully understand how you are one God in three persons – but we trust what Jesus has shown us. Draw us deeper into knowing you – Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. Amen.

chapters.chapterTitle: What is the Problem? – Sin and the Human Heart

elements.question: It is worth reading all three passages before you begin. Together they build a picture of what the Bible means by sin – and why it matters so much.
elements.answer:
Mark 7:14-23

14Then he called the crowd again and said to them, ‘Listen to me, everyone, and understand. 15There is nothing outside of a person that can defile him by going into him. Rather, it is what comes out of a person that defiles him.’ 17Now when he had left the crowd and entered the house, his disciples asked him about the parable. 18He said to them, ‘Are you so foolish? Don’t you understand that whatever goes into a person from outside cannot defile him? 19For it does not enter his heart but his stomach, and then goes out into the sewer.’ (This means all foods are clean.) 20He said, ‘What comes out of a person is what defiles him. 21For from within, out of the human heart, come evil ideas, sexual immorality, theft, murder, 22adultery, greed, evil, deceit, debauchery, envy, slander, pride, and folly. 23All these evils come from within and defile a person.’


Romans 3:9-24

9What then? Are we better off? Certainly not, for we have already charged that Jews and Greeks alike are all under sin, 10just as it is written: ‘There is no one righteous, not even one, 11there is no one who understands, there is no one who seeks God. 12All have turned away, together they have become worthless; there is no one who shows kindness, not even one.’ 13‘Their throats are open graves, they deceive with their tongues, the poison of vipers is under their lips.’ 14‘Their mouths are full of cursing and bitterness.’ 15‘Their feet are swift to shed blood, 16ruin and misery are in their paths, 17and the way of peace they have not known.’ 18‘There is no fear of God before their eyes.’ 19Now we know that whatever the law says, it says to those who are under the law, so that every mouth may be silenced and the whole world may be held accountable to God. 20For no one is declared righteous before him by the works of the law, for through the law comes the knowledge of sin. 21But now apart from the law the righteousness of God (which is attested by the law and the prophets) has been disclosed– 22namely, the righteousness of God through the faithfulness of Jesus Christ for all who believe. For there is no distinction, 23for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God. 24But they are justified freely by his grace through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus.’


Isaiah 59:1-2

1Look, the Lord’s hand is not too weak to deliver you, nor is his ear too deaf to hear you. 2But your sinful deeds have built a barrier between you and your God. Your sins have made him hide his face from you so that he does not hear.’


Mark 7 (NET – see context ↗)  |  Romans 3 (NET – see context ↗)  |  Isaiah 59 (NET – see context ↗)

elements.text:
Imagine a doctor who tells you your symptoms are minor – that you simply need to rest more and try harder. You follow the advice. But the pain keeps returning, because the doctor missed the real diagnosis. The problem was never your behaviour. It was something deeper that no amount of effort could fix.

Many people who have taken their faith seriously describe exactly this kind of restlessness – a sense that something is unresolved even after sincere religious practice. They prayed faithfully, fasted carefully, tried to obey diligently – and still something felt wrong at a level that effort couldn’t reach. If that is part of your experience, the Bible has a name for it. The problem is not a failure of devotion. It is that the diagnosis was incomplete.

That is what this chapter is for. Many of us arrive with a deeply ingrained instinct: the fundamental problem between us and God is what we do – specific sins, particular failures. And the solution, it follows, must be to do better: pray more, resist more, discipline ourselves more strictly.

But the Bible tells a different story. The problem is not simply what we do. It is what we are – what has gone wrong at the very centre of who we are. Before the gospel can be good news, we need to understand clearly what it is good news about.
Part 1: Where Does Evil Come From?

Jesus was once asked about purity – specifically about whether eating certain foods with unwashed hands made a person spiritually unclean. It was a serious religious question, and the people asking it had devoted their lives to getting the answer right. Jesus’ response surprised everyone in the room. He turned the entire question inside out.

elements.passageText: <sup>14</sup>Then he called the crowd again and said to them, ‘Listen to me, everyone, and understand. <sup>15</sup>There is nothing outside of a person that can defile him by going into him. Rather, it is what comes out of a person that defiles him.’ <sup>17</sup>Now when he had left the crowd and entered the house, his disciples asked him about the parable. <sup>18</sup>He said to them, ‘Are you so foolish? Don’t you understand that whatever goes into a person from outside cannot defile him? <sup>19</sup>For it does not enter his heart but his stomach, and then goes out into the sewer.’ (This means all foods are clean.) <sup>20</sup>He said, ‘What comes out of a person is what defiles him. <sup>21</sup>For from within, out of the human heart, come evil ideas, sexual immorality, theft, murder, <sup>22</sup>adultery, greed, evil, deceit, debauchery, envy, slander, pride, and folly. <sup>23</sup>All these evils come from within and defile a person.’

elements.question: Jesus says that what defiles a person comes not from outside but from within – from the human heart. Why is this diagnosis more serious than simply having a list of rules we need to follow more carefully?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: If the source of defilement were outside us – in food, in contact with unclean things, in breaking specific rules – then the solution would simply be to follow the rules more carefully and avoid the wrong things. But Jesus locates the problem inside the human heart. The list in verses 21-22 – evil ideas, greed, deceit, envy, pride – flows out of what we already are. This means the problem cannot be fixed by better external behaviour, because the heart that produces these things remains unchanged. No set of rules, however carefully followed, can clean the source from which the pollution comes.
elements.questionHint: If the problem were outside you — in the food, the environment, or the people around you — what would the solution look like, and how would that differ from a problem that comes from inside you?

elements.question: Look at the things Jesus says come from the human heart: evil ideas, greed, deceit, envy, pride, and more. Some of these are actions – but many of them are attitudes. What does it mean that these internal attitudes are included in Jesus’ definition of defilement?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus is saying that defilement is not only about what a person does but about what they harbour inside. Envy, pride, and deceit are not primarily actions – they are orientations of the heart that may never produce a visible forbidden act but are still, in Jesus’ definition, corrupting. This means that a person who has never committed an obviously serious sin may still be deeply defiled, because defilement is a condition of the inner life. It closes off the possibility of using outward compliance as a measure of spiritual health.
elements.questionHint: Could a person avoid every item on that list as an outward action and yet still harbour envy or pride in their heart — and if so, what does Jesus’ inclusion of those attitudes tell you about where the real problem lies?

elements.question: When you hear the word sin, what comes to mind? A list of forbidden actions? A scale of serious and minor offences? Think about your instinctive definition before reading on.

elements.text: Part 2: No One is Exempt

If Jesus’ teaching in Mark 7 identifies where the problem is – inside the human heart – then Paul’s letter to the Romans identifies how far it extends. His argument is sustained and deeply uncomfortable: the problem is not limited to the obviously wicked. It reaches everyone. The religious and the irreligious. The devout and the careless. The well-intentioned and the openly rebellious.

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>What then? Are we better off? Certainly not, for we have already charged that Jews and Greeks alike are all under sin, <sup>10</sup>just as it is written: ‘There is no one righteous, not even one, <sup>11</sup>there is no one who understands, there is no one who seeks God. <sup>12</sup>All have turned away, together they have become worthless; there is no one who shows kindness, not even one.’ <sup>13</sup>‘Their throats are open graves, they deceive with their tongues, the poison of vipers is under their lips.’ <sup>14</sup>‘Their mouths are full of cursing and bitterness.’ <sup>15</sup>‘Their feet are swift to shed blood, <sup>16</sup>ruin and misery are in their paths, <sup>17</sup>and the way of peace they have not known.’ <sup>18</sup>‘There is no fear of God before their eyes.’ <sup>19</sup>Now we know that whatever the law says, it says to those who are under the law, so that every mouth may be silenced and the whole world may be held accountable to God. <sup>20</sup>For no one is declared righteous before him by the works of the law, for through the law comes the knowledge of sin. <sup>21</sup>But now apart from the law the righteousness of God (which is attested by the law and the prophets) has been disclosed– <sup>22</sup>namely, the righteousness of God through the faithfulness of Jesus Christ for all who believe. For there is no distinction, <sup>23</sup>for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God. <sup>24</sup>But they are justified freely by his grace through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus.’

elements.question: Paul quotes a string of Old Testament passages and applies them to everyone – not just the most obviously sinful. Why do you think Paul is so insistent that there is “no distinction” (verse 22)? What is he trying to close off?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul is closing off the escape route that says some people – the devout, the morally careful, the religiously observant – are in a different category and do not need what the gospel offers. He has already argued in the earlier chapters of Romans that both Jews and Gentiles are under sin, and here he seals that argument: every mouth is silenced, the whole world is accountable. By insisting there is no distinction, Paul ensures that the righteousness of God through Jesus is not a solution only for the obviously wicked but for every human being without exception – including those who would be most tempted to think their religious effort had earned them a different standing.
elements.questionHint: Who in Paul’s original audience would have been most tempted to think they were in a different category — and what argument does he make in verses 9 and 19 to close off that possibility?

elements.question: Verse 20 says that the law – religious commands and rules – can diagnose sin, but cannot cure it. What does this suggest about the limits of religious observance on its own?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says that through the law comes the knowledge of sin – the law shows us what the standard is and therefore reveals how far short we fall. But it cannot declare anyone righteous. This suggests that religious observance, however thorough and sincere, is functioning as a mirror rather than a cure: it shows you the problem but cannot fix it. A person who tries harder to obey religious rules in response to the law’s diagnosis is like a patient who studies their test results more carefully but takes no medicine. The diagnosis may become clearer, but the condition remains. The cure, Paul says, must come from somewhere other than the law.
elements.questionHint: Think about the difference between a tool that can show you a problem and a tool that can fix it — what does verse 20 say the law is able to do, and what does it conspicuously not say it can do?

elements.question: Paul uses the phrase “fall short of the glory of God” (verse 23). Sin is not simply breaking rules – it is a falling short of something. What do you think humanity was created to be? And what does it mean to fall short of that?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The phrase implies that human beings were made for something – not merely to avoid wrongdoing but to reflect or participate in the glory of God. To fall short is not primarily to break a rule but to fail to be what we were designed to be. It is the language of a missed destination rather than a crossed line. This reframes sin: it is not just transgression but diminishment – a failure to reach the fullness of what a human life was meant to express. The problem is not merely guilt for specific acts but a condition of fallenness in which the whole direction of human life has veered away from its created purpose.

deeper.label: Go deeper…
deeper.question: Genesis 1:27 says that human beings were made in the image of God – to reflect his character, his love, his justice. If that is what we were made for, how does that change the way we understand sin? Is it primarily about rule-breaking, or about something more fundamental?

elements.questionHint: Notice that Paul uses the language of ‘falling short’ of something rather than ‘crossing a line’ — what does that image suggest about what human beings were originally designed to reach or reflect?

elements.text: Notice that Paul does not end at verse 23. He continues immediately into verse 24: “But they are justified freely by his grace through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus.” The diagnosis of sin is not the last word. It is the necessary preparation for the gospel. A doctor must name the illness before the medicine makes sense. Paul names the illness clearly and unflinchingly – precisely because the cure is extraordinary, and it must be received, not earned.
Part 3: The Separation Sin Creates

So far we have seen that sin comes from inside the human heart, and that it extends to all people without exception. But what does sin actually do? Is it simply a moral accounting problem – a debt that accumulates and must eventually be settled? The prophet Isaiah goes deeper than that.

Many people describe a religious life in which prayer felt like it was going nowhere – as though God was not listening, as though they were speaking into silence. Isaiah names that experience directly and gives it a theological explanation.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Look, the Lord’s hand is not too weak to deliver you, nor is his ear too deaf to hear you. <sup>2</sup>But your sinful deeds have built a barrier between you and your God. Your sins have made him hide his face from you so that he does not hear.’

elements.question: Isaiah makes something clear: the problem is not that God is unable to reach us, or unwilling to hear us. The problem is on our side. Sin has created a separation – an alienation. What does it feel like to be alienated from someone you were made to be close to?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Isaiah carefully removes two possible explanations: God’s hand is not too short, and his ear is not too deaf. He is neither incapable nor inattentive. The barrier is on the human side – sin has built a wall between the person and God. This means the silence in prayer, the sense that God is not present or not responding, is not evidence that God is distant by nature or indifferent. It is the consequence of a real separation caused by sin. Alienation from someone you were made to be close to involves a loss of the relationship itself – not merely its absence but the pain of something that should be there and is not.
elements.questionHint: Notice that Isaiah rules out two possible explanations for why prayer can feel unheard — what are those two explanations, and what does that leave as the only remaining cause?

elements.question: Isaiah says God’s hand is ‘not too short to deliver’ – he is fully capable of rescuing. So if God is capable and the problem lies with us, what kind of solution would actually be needed? What would have to happen for the separation to be bridged?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: If God is fully capable and the problem lies entirely on the human side – in the barrier that sin has built – then the solution cannot come from human effort, since that effort is itself part of the problem. What would be needed is for God, from his side, to do something about the barrier. The separation is real and structural, not merely a misunderstanding to be cleared up. Bridging it would require dealing with sin itself – not ignoring it, which would leave the barrier in place, but removing it in a way that satisfies the reality of what it is. The solution would have to come from God’s initiative, not ours.
elements.questionHint: If the barrier was built by sin on our side, and human effort is itself part of the problem, which side of the barrier would the solution need to come from — and what would that require of God?

elements.text: A Framework: Two Ways of Understanding the Problem

It may help to set two contrasting understandings of sin alongside each other – not to argue, but to see clearly what the Bible is actually saying.

A common instinct:
The human being is essentially capable – we know what is right; we simply lose sight of it and need to be reminded. Sin is forgetfulness, drifting, a failure to obey clearly enough. The fix, therefore, is to try harder, do more, discipline yourself more carefully. If the problem is forgetting, then the solution is reminding. A prophet who speaks God’s words and calls us back to him makes perfect sense as the solution. With enough effort and the right guidance, the gap can be closed.

What the Bible teaches:
Sin is a condition of the heart – a disordering of who we are at our deepest level. It is not simply that we forget God from time to time; it is that something has gone wrong at the root. We are not just forgetful – we are, as Romans 3 puts it, unable to seek God at all on our own. Because the problem runs that deep, the solution must come from outside us entirely. A broken compass cannot be fixed by giving it better directions – it needs to be repaired from the inside. That is why what is needed is not a reminder but a rescue, not a messenger pointing the way, but a saviour who carries us there.

A life of careful obedience – where each act must be correct, where the outcome is never certain until the end – is an exhausting place to live. The Bible does not dismiss that effort. But it offers something different: not a higher standard to reach, but a gift to receive.

This is not meant to discourage. It is meant to liberate. When we see clearly that we cannot save ourselves, we are ready to receive the salvation that God offers as a gift.

elements.question: Many people have lived deeply devout, disciplined, sincere religious lives. Does the Bible’s teaching on sin denigrate that effort – or is it making a different kind of point? What is the difference between sincerity and sufficiency?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your thoughts…

elements.text: Common Questions

elements.term: “But surely God rewards those who try hard and are sincere?”
elements.question: If God sees our effort and sincerity, doesn’t that count for something?
elements.answer: Yes – God sees effort, sincerity, and genuine devotion. The Bible never dismisses these things. But Romans 3 is making a different and more fundamental point: that no one, however sincere or devout, fully meets the standard of God’s perfect holiness. That gap – between our best and God’s perfection – is precisely the problem the gospel addresses. The issue is not that God fails to notice our sincerity. The issue is that sincerity alone cannot bridge a gap that runs to the very root of our nature. What is needed is not more effort but a new kind of righteousness – one that comes from God himself. That is what Paul introduces in verse 21: “the righteousness of God... through the faithfulness of Jesus Christ.”

elements.term: “I have prayed sincerely, fasted, and tried to live correctly. Why isn’t that enough?”
elements.question: Many people have performed all the required religious duties faithfully – and still found no lasting peace. Why isn’t sincere religious practice sufficient?
elements.answer:
The Bible’s answer here is not that those things don’t matter – they reflect real devotion and a genuine desire to know God. But Romans 3 is pointing to something deeper: that the problem is not a shortfall in effort, but a condition of the heart that effort alone cannot fix. Think of it this way: washing the outside of a cup does not clean what is on the inside. No amount of external obedience can reach the internal disorder that Jesus describes in Mark 7.

This is not meant to discourage. It is meant to explain why, even after sincere effort, something still feels unresolved – and to point toward the only solution that actually reaches deep enough. The good news is that the solution is not something we achieve. It is something we receive.

elements.term: “Is sin really that serious? Isn’t God merciful enough to overlook it?”
elements.question: If God is merciful and compassionate, why can’t he simply forgive and move on?
elements.answer: God is merciful – extraordinarily, overwhelmingly so. But Isaiah 59:1-2 shows that the issue is not God’s willingness to forgive; it is the reality of what sin does. It creates a separation – an alienation – that cannot simply be wished away without something real happening to deal with it. Think of it this way: a judge who overlooks every crime in the name of mercy is not a good judge – he is an unjust one. True mercy and true justice must both be satisfied. This is exactly what the cross achieves: it is the place where God’s justice and his mercy meet. Sin is serious because the separation it causes is real. But the good news is that God himself is the one who bridges it – at great cost to himself.

elements.term: “Why does Jesus need to be a saviour? Couldn’t a great prophet be enough?”
elements.question: Many traditions honour Jesus as a great prophet. Why do Christians insist he must be more than that?
elements.answer:
The answer depends entirely on what you think the problem is. If the problem between us and God is mainly that people have forgotten him – that we drift and need to be called back – then a prophet makes perfect sense. The prophet speaks God’s words, calls people back to God, and shows the way. And it is true that human beings do forget God, and that this matters.

But the Bible’s diagnosis goes deeper. It is not just that we forget God – it is that something has gone wrong at the root of who we are. Romans 3 says we are unable to seek God at all on our own. A broken compass cannot be fixed by giving it better directions – it needs to be repaired from the inside. That is why the Bible says what is needed is not a reminder but a rescue; not a messenger pointing the way, but a saviour who carries us there.

This is the logic of the cross. Jesus did not come primarily to teach us better – he came to do for us what we could not do for ourselves.

elements.term: “Does this mean all people are equally sinful? What about genuine evil-doers compared to ordinary people?”
elements.question: Surely there are degrees of sin? Paul’s “no distinction” seems too sweeping.
elements.answer: Paul is not saying that all actions are equally harmful or that there are no moral distinctions between people. Clearly some acts are more destructive than others. His point is different: that in terms of our standing before God’s perfect holiness, no one has achieved what they were made for. Every human being, however outwardly good, falls short of the glory of God. It is like the difference between someone who swims halfway across a wide ocean and someone who barely leaves the shore – they are genuinely different in ability, but both are unable to reach the other side. The solution for both is the same: they need to be rescued. Paul’s universal diagnosis is not meant to flatten all moral difference; it is meant to close off the escape route that says, “I am good enough not to need what Jesus offers.”

elements.text: Personal Reflection

This chapter has covered difficult ground – the kind of ground that requires honesty. The Bible’s diagnosis of sin is not comfortable, but it is not meant to leave you in despair. Every great doctor who gives a hard diagnosis does so because there is a treatment. We have glimpsed that treatment in Paul’s words – “justified freely by his grace” – and we will explore it fully in the chapters ahead. For now, take a moment to sit with what this chapter has surfaced.

elements.instruction: Choose the option that best reflects where you are right now. There are no right or wrong answers.
statements: I have a clearer understanding of what the Bible means by sin after this chapter.
I find it challenging – but helpful – that the Bible says no one is exempt from the problem of sin.
Knowing that the problem is deeper than behaviour makes me more open to the idea that the solution must come from God.

elements.question: Before this chapter, did you think of sin primarily as a list of actions – or as something deeper? Has anything shifted in how you see it?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: Isaiah speaks of sin creating a separation between us and God. Have you ever experienced that sense of distance – a feeling that your prayers were going nowhere, that something remained unresolved despite your sincerity? What did that feel like?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: Paul ends his diagnosis with a glimpse of the solution: “justified freely by his grace.” What questions does that raise for you as you look ahead?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.text: The Bible’s teaching on sin is honest – even brutal in its thoroughness. But it is honest in the way that a good doctor is honest: not to wound, but to heal. The diagnosis of the human heart is not the last word. It is the first word of a story that ends in rescue, in forgiveness, in a righteousness that comes not from our effort but from God’s grace. We have seen the problem clearly in this chapter. In the next, we will see the extraordinary lengths to which God went to solve it – in the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus.

elements.question: This week, read Psalm 51 – the prayer of King David after a serious moral failure. Notice how he describes his condition: it is not merely about what he did, but about what he is. And notice what he asks for: he does not ask God to remind him more firmly or send him a better teacher. He asks God to “create in me a clean heart” – to do something that only God himself can do. What does this tell you about the kind of God we are dealing with?
Use this prayer to close your time together, or let it guide your own words.
elements.answer: Lord God, we come honestly before you. We have seen today that the problem between us and you is not something we can fix by trying harder or doing more. The problem runs deeper than our actions – it runs to the heart. We cannot bridge the gap ourselves. But we trust that you have not left us on the other side. Thank you that your hand is not too short to reach us. We look to you – and we look ahead to seeing what you have done in Jesus. Amen.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Knowing God through Jesus (vol 1): A Bible Study Series for New Believers
studyOnboardingSlides.body:
A five-session Bible study designed for people from a Muslim background who have recently come to Christ.

Work through each chapter at your own pace, or use it with a group.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Read, reflect, write
studyOnboardingSlides.body:
Each chapter has Bible questions tied to specific passages, and personal reflection questions at the end.

Tap ⓘ beside any Bible reference to read the passage without leaving the app.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Saved on your device
studyOnboardingSlides.body:
Your answers are saved automatically as you type, and stored on this device.

Use the Share button at the bottom of each chapter to send your answers to a group leader or mentor.
