blocks.title: New believers from a Muslim background
blocks.body: This series is written specifically for people who have recently come to Christ from a Muslim background. It assumes you are not starting from scratch – you already know much about God, Scripture, and how to live a devout life. But it also recognises that coming to faith in Jesus involves rebuilding a framework, not just adding new information. These ten sessions are designed to help you do that carefully, at a pace that gives you room to think and to ask hard questions.

blocks.title: People carrying real costs
blocks.body: Many who work through this study will have paid – or will be paying – a significant personal cost for following Jesus: in family relationships, community standing, or personal safety. This series does not minimise that. From Session 0 onwards, it is built around a simple conviction: you belong here, and the God who has drawn you to himself will hold you.

blocks.title: Leaders and mentors walking alongside
blocks.body: If you are leading someone through this study – as a pastor, a small group leader, or a trusted friend – the material is designed to be unhurried and relational. The goal is not to win arguments or cover content quickly. It is to help participants encounter Jesus through Scripture, in a space where deep questions are expected and genuinely welcomed.

blocks.title: On your own
blocks.body: This study can be worked through individually. Read the Bible passages, work through the Explore questions, and spend time with the Reflection Questions at the end of each session. There is no right speed. Some sessions – particularly those on the Trinity, the cross, and new identity – may take longer than others. That is expected and good.

blocks.title: In a group
blocks.body: The study is designed to work especially well in a small, trusted group of people on a similar journey. Session 0 is a gathering session with no teaching content at all – its only purpose is to establish safety and belonging before anything else begins. In a group setting, the Common Questions sections can open up real conversation. The goal is not right answers given quickly, but honest thinking done together.

blocks.title: Working with a leader or mentor
blocks.body:
Having a trusted guide through this series makes a significant difference – someone who can sit with hard questions, make space for honest doubt, and offer both theological grounding and pastoral care. The best leaders are those who have worked through the material themselves before leading others through it.

At the end of each session, the Share button lets you send your written answers directly to your leader or mentor – via WhatsApp, email, or any other app on your phone. This gives them a window into your thinking before you meet, and makes for much richer conversation.

blocks.title: Two kinds of questions
blocks.body: Each session has two kinds of questions. The Explore questions are tied to specific Bible passages – asking you to look carefully at what the text actually says. The Reflection Questions at the end are more personal – asking what the session means for you specifically, and what you want to carry into the week ahead.

blocks.title: Common Questions
blocks.body: Each session includes a Common Questions section addressing the specific objections and struggles that often arise – about the reliability of the Bible, the Trinity, the crucifixion, grace versus works, and more. These are not there to shut down conversation but to open it up. If a question in that section connects with something you are wrestling with, bring it to your group or mentor.

leadersNotesData.subtitle: Knowing God Through Jesus
intro: These notes are designed to help group leaders and mentors get the most out of each session – both for the people they are leading and for themselves. They are not a substitute for working through the material yourself first. The best leaders are those who have sat with each session personally before helping others through it.

Know your group’s context. This series has been written with people from Muslim backgrounds in mind – people who may be carrying real risk because of their faith. Some in your group may not be fully ’out’ about their belief in Jesus. Some may be navigating family pressure, community suspicion, or genuine danger. Hold that reality gently but constantly.

Create safety before you create depth. The questions in this study are designed to prompt honest reflection, not correct answers. But honest reflection will only happen if the group genuinely feels safe. Return to the confidentiality agreement from Session 0 as often as needed. Remind people that what is shared here stays here.

Don’t be afraid of the hard questions. This series addresses some of the most significant objections a person from a Muslim background might bring to Christian faith: the corruption of the Bible, the Trinity and shirk, the crucifixion, the meaning of ’Son of God’. These are not embarrassing questions. They are serious ones, and they deserve serious answers. Prepare for them. Welcome them when they arise.

Watch for the person carrying the most. In a group like this, some people will be processing things that go far beyond theology – grief over family rejection, fear about what comes next, loneliness. The most important thing you can do is notice, and not rush past it.

keyPoints.body:
This opening session sets the tone for everything that follows. Its primary purpose is not information transfer – it is belonging creation. Before anyone feels safe enough to wrestle with hard theological questions, they need to feel that they are genuinely welcome, that their story matters, and that this is a space where honesty is possible.

Psalm 23 is chosen deliberately as the opening passage: it is ancient, it is cross-cultural, and it speaks of God as one who pursues, restores, and accompanies – images that resonate across traditions. Let it breathe. Don’t rush past it into content.

The confidentiality agreement is not a formality – it is the foundation on which the whole series rests. For people who are not ’out’ about their faith, the knowledge that nothing shared will leave the room is not a nice-to-have. It is essential. Establish it clearly and return to it if needed in later sessions.

The series overview callout gives the group a map. Use it to normalise the journey – ten sessions is a significant commitment, and helping people see where they are going increases the chance that they stay the course.

pastorals.body:
The three opening questions – where you’re from, your journey to faith, a word for how you feel – are deceptively simple. For some in the group, even the second question will be emotionally charged. ’My journey to faith is still very new’ and ’my journey is complicated and I’m not sure I should be here’ are both entirely valid answers. Make sure the group knows that.

Watch for people who describe themselves as ’not sure yet’ – this is the most common honest answer, and it’s the right one to encourage. The closing question (‘What do you hope to discover through this series?’) is especially important: it begins to build ownership and gives you pastoral intelligence about where each person is.

The prayer at the close is gentle and explicitly accommodating of people who find praying out loud uncomfortable. Use it as written, or adapt it – but end every session in prayer. It models what the series is trying to build: honest conversation with God.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for people who are present because someone else brought them – who are not yet sure they want to be there. Make space for that without drawing attention to it. The goal of Session 0 is simply that they come back for Session 1.

chapters.title: How Can We Know God? Trusting the Bible

keyPoints.body:
This session addresses what is often the first and most significant barrier for those coming from a Muslim background: can the Bible be trusted? The ’corruption of the Scriptures’ claim is deeply embedded in Islamic teaching, and it needs a direct, respectful, and well-reasoned response – not a dismissal.

The session builds its case carefully: God is a God who speaks (Hebrews 1:1-4); the Bible is God-breathed (2 Timothy 3:14-17); the Old and New Testaments form one unified story centred on Jesus (Luke 24:44-47). These three moves together form the theological architecture of the answer.

The misunderstanding callout on corruption is the most important content in the session. The manuscript evidence argument – over 5,800 Greek New Testament manuscripts, with variations that affect no central doctrine – is accessible and compelling. The more powerful point is the chronological one: if the corruption happened after Jesus, the manuscripts we have predate the supposed corruption. This is worth dwelling on.

The recommendation to read Mark’s Gospel in one or two sittings is excellent practical advice. For a new believer, the experience of encountering Jesus on the page – directly, without mediation – is often transformative.

pastorals.body:
Be prepared for the corruption question to come up regardless of the session’s shape – it is the question most people in this group are carrying. Have your answer internalised, not just read off a callout. And hold it with confidence but not aggression: the person asking it is not being difficult; they were taught something, and they deserve a thoughtful response.

The Likert scale at the end of the session (‘I feel confident opening the Bible on my own’) will surface people who are still deeply uncertain. Don’t treat low scores as failure – treat them as pastoral information. A person who ends this session with ’I’m not sure I believe it yet but I want to keep reading’ is in exactly the right place.

The application challenge – read Mark 1-4 in one sitting – is worth pressing. Following up at the start of Session 2 with ’Did you read it? What did you notice?’ both holds the group accountable and creates momentum.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for people who have been taught that engaging with the Bible is itself spiritually dangerous – a form of corruption or compromise. For them, the act of opening it is an act of courage. Honour that, even if they don’t name it.

chapters.title: Who is God? The Personal God

keyPoints.body:
This session addresses the picture of God that people carry before – and often after – coming to faith. For many from Muslim backgrounds, God is primarily sovereign, transcendent, and remote: al-Kabir (the Great), al-Aziz (the Mighty). These things are true and important. But the session argues that the Bible adds something more: God is also intimate, near, and personally knowable.

Psalm 103 is the pastoral heart of the session. Its insistence on God’s compassion – that he ’knows we are made of clay’ and removes sin as far as east from west – is exactly the kind of God that exhausted, frightened, or shame-laden people need to encounter. Do not rush through it.

John 1:1-18 makes the staggering move: the Logos who was God ’tabernacled’ among us. The deliberate use of the tabernacle image (the word translated ’dwelt’) connects the Incarnation to God’s presence among his people in the wilderness. This is an important connection for those who know the Old Testament tradition.

The misunderstanding callout on ’Is the God of the Bible the same as Allah?’ deserves careful preparation. The honest answer is: same creator God – but a much fuller and more surprising revelation. This is not a dismissal of Islamic teaching; it is an invitation to go further.

pastorals.body:
The opening question – ‘How would you describe your picture of God before you came to faith in Jesus?’ – will surface a wide range of answers. Some people will describe a God they feared. Some a God they could never fully please. Some a God who felt absent. All of these are worth receiving without correction before the session’s passages begin to do their work.

The Lord’s Prayer section may prompt discomfort at calling God ’Father’ – the misunderstanding callout on this is important. Abba is not casual; it is the language of secure, trusting intimacy. Make sure that distinction is clear before moving on.

The Likert scale – ‘I find it natural to think of God as personally close to me’ – will almost certainly surface low scores from some in the group. That is a normal response at this stage. Affirm that the Christian life is a journey into this reality, not a starting requirement.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for people whose picture of God has been formed primarily by fear or by performance anxiety. The session’s central claim – that God wants to be known by you – may feel almost unbelievable to them. Let that land slowly. Don’t push for acceptance; trust the passages.

chapters.title: Who is Jesus? (Part 1) – Son of God and Lord

keyPoints.body:
This is the session where the identity question becomes unavoidable. The series so far has introduced God and the Bible; now it faces the question that divides Christianity from Islam at the deepest level: who is Jesus, exactly?

Mark’s Gospel does the heavy lifting here. Mark 1:1-11 presents Jesus through three voices – the narrator (v.1), John the Baptist (vv.7-8), and God himself (v.11) – before Jesus has said a single public word. The effect is deliberate: the reader is placed in the position of the crowd, already confronted with the question of who this is.

Mark 2:1-12 is theologically decisive. The teachers of the law are right in their logic: only God can forgive sins. Jesus’ act forces a conclusion – either he is blaspheming, or he is God. He allows no comfortable middle position. The ’Son of Man’ title (from Daniel 7) raises the stakes further: this is the figure to whom God gives eternal dominion over all nations.

Colossians 1:15-20 gives the theological synthesis: Jesus is the image of the invisible God, the agent of creation, the firstborn from the dead, the one who holds all things together. This is not a modest claim, and the session does not soften it.

pastorals.body:
The misunderstanding callout on ’The Bible never says I am God, worship me’ is essential pastoral preparation. The objection will be raised. Your answer needs to be both accurate and accessible: Jesus made the claim through actions, titles, and patterns of speech that his first-century Jewish audience understood perfectly – and that is why they wanted to stone him.

Some group members may feel that accepting Jesus as more than a prophet is a betrayal of everything they have been taught. This is not an intellectual objection – it is an identity-level disruption. Hold space for it. Do not push for resolution in a single session.

The question ’Who do you say that I am?’ (Mark 8:29) is left as the session’s implicit close. Don’t force an answer. Let people sit with it as a genuine, personal question. The Spirit does his own work here.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for people who are intellectually persuaded but emotionally resistant – who can follow the argument but find the conclusion costly. That gap between head and heart is real and it takes time to close. Don’t confuse intellectual acknowledgement with genuine faith.

chapters.title: Who is Jesus? (Part 2) – Understanding the Trinity

keyPoints.body:
This session addresses the doctrine that most people from Muslim backgrounds find most challenging: the Trinity. The session is careful to frame it correctly from the outset – the Trinity is not three gods, but one God who has eternally existed in a relationship of Father, Son, and Spirit.

John 14:1-17 is the primary text. In it, Jesus describes his relationship with the Father (v.9: ‘Whoever has seen me has seen the Father’), promises the Spirit (vv.16-17), and speaks of the three in a pattern that is Trinitarian before anyone had coined the term. This is the session’s best evidence against the ’Nicaean invention’ objection: Jesus himself spoke this way.

The three misunderstanding callouts are the most important content in the session and deserve preparation time: the shirk objection, the Nicaea objection, and the ’Son of God implies something physical’ objection. Each is answered clearly and respectfully. Know these answers in your own words before the group meets.

The closing text is theologically rich: the Trinity is not a puzzle but a relationship opened wide – Father, Son, and Spirit already in eternal communion, and the gospel as an invitation into that communion. Let that vision land.

pastorals.body:
The shirk question is the pastoral centre of this session. It must be engaged with genuine respect – the concern behind it is right and good. Christians agree that associating created beings with God is a grave error. The Trinity is not that: it is the revealed nature of the one God. Say this clearly and without defensiveness.

Expect some group members to find this session the most intellectually difficult of the series. The closing bridge text is a gift here: ‘You do not need to fully understand a person before you can know them.’ People can hold the Trinity as a mystery-in-progress and still know and love the God who has revealed himself in Jesus.

The application challenge – reading John 14 slowly, noticing every mention of Father, Son, and Spirit – is one of the most formative exercises in the series. Encourage it warmly and follow up at the start of the next session.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for people who find Trinitarian language genuinely threatening to their faith or their sense of integrity. These are not academic objections – they are coming from somewhere deep. Give them the space to say ’I’m not there yet’ without that feeling like failure.

chapters.title: What is the Problem? Sin and the Human Heart

keyPoints.body:
This session lays the diagnostic groundwork for the gospel. Before the cross can be heard as good news, the group needs to understand what it is good news about. The session’s central move is to reframe sin: not primarily as a list of forbidden acts, but as a condition of the human heart (Mark 7:20-23) that reaches every person without exception (Romans 3:9-24).

The doctor illustration in the introduction is deliberately accessible. The problem is not that the prescription is wrong – it is that the diagnosis is wrong. We cannot fix an interior condition with exterior effort. This reframing is essential preparation for understanding grace.

Isaiah 59:1-2 adds the relational dimension: sin creates a separation between God and humanity. God’s hand is not too short to save – the separation is not a problem of God’s power, but of humanity’s condition. This sets up the cross as the only possible resolution.

The session does not end in condemnation. Romans 3:21-24 – ‘But now apart from the law the righteousness of God has been disclosed’ – is the dawn breaking at the end of a long diagnosis. Don’t let the group leave without landing there.

pastorals.body:
For people from backgrounds of careful religious practice, the claim that sin runs deeper than behaviour is genuinely disorienting. It feels like the rules are being moved. Be patient with the disorientation. The point is not that obedience doesn’t matter – it is that obedience cannot reach the root of the problem.

The question about the list in Mark 7 – that many of the items are attitudes, not actions – is a key pastoral moment. Pride, envy, and deceit are not dramatic public sins; they are quiet interior ones. This often produces unexpected honesty in a group, and unexpected conviction. Don’t rush past it.

Watch for people who come to this session carrying shame about specific sins. The session is not designed to increase that shame – it is designed to universalise the diagnosis so that no one is more condemned than anyone else, and to point everyone toward the same rescue.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for people who respond to ’all have sinned’ with a performance comparison – ‘but I have sinned less than most.’ Paul’s argument in Romans 3 is specifically designed to close that exit. Help the group see that the universality of the diagnosis is actually freeing: it means everyone needs the same rescue, and the same rescue is available to everyone.

chapters.title: What Did Jesus Do? The Cross and the Resurrection

keyPoints.body:
This is the theological centre of the series. Sessions 5-7 form a sequence: diagnosis (sin), solution (the cross and resurrection), and application (grace through faith). This session provides the solution, and it must land with full weight.

The four passages work together: Mark 10:45 (Jesus came to give his life as a ransom); Isaiah 53:4-6 (the suffering servant bearing our iniquities – a remarkable Old Testament anticipation); Romans 5:6-8 (Christ died for us while we were still sinners – the timing is everything); 1 Corinthians 15:17-20 (if Christ has not been raised, the whole thing collapses – but he has been raised).

The resurrection is Paul’s main argument in 1 Corinthians 15. It is not a postscript to the cross – it is God’s declaration that the cross worked. The tomb was empty; the witnesses were many and named; the disciples were transformed. This is historical argument, not merely theological assertion.

The session includes detailed leader notes for three anticipated questions: the Quranic claim that Jesus was not crucified, why God would allow a prophet to die this way, and how we know the resurrection happened. These are essential preparation – study them carefully before the session.

pastorals.body:
The claim that Jesus was not crucified is the objection most likely to be raised by someone in the group, and it requires confident but respectful engagement. The historical evidence for the crucifixion is extraordinary – Roman and Jewish sources outside the Bible, the empty tomb, the transformation of the disciples. The question is not whether it happened, but what it meant.

The reflection callout – ‘God did this for you, while you were still far from him’ – is meant to be a moment of stillness. Let it be. Don’t fill it with more content.

The three reflection questions at the end of the session are among the most personally significant in the series. Give them generous time. The question ’What would it mean to live as someone already forgiven, already accepted, already held?’ is one that many in this group – carrying shame, fear, and the weight of what they have left behind – need to sit with quietly.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for people who accept the cross intellectually but cannot feel its personal application. ’Christ died for sinners’ is easy to assent to; ’Christ died for me, specifically, while I was still far from him’ is where the gospel becomes real. Gently hold that distinction throughout.

chapters.title: How Are We Saved? Grace, Not Works

keyPoints.body:
This session applies the gospel directly to the question of how salvation is received. For many in this group, the deep instinct is that God’s acceptance must be earned – through prayer, fasting, obedience, effort. The session does not dismiss this instinct dismissively; it takes it seriously and then shows why it falls short.

Ephesians 2:1-10 is the theological anchor. The movement of the passage is crucial: we were dead (vv.1-3), but God – ‘being rich in mercy, because of his great love’ (v.4) – acted. The subject changes from ’us’ to ’God.’ This is the grammar of grace. Verse 8 makes it explicit: ‘by grace you are saved through faith, and this is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God.’ Verse 10 guards against the misreading: good works are still essential, but they flow from salvation rather than achieving it.

The parable in Luke 18:9-14 is Jesus at his most deliberately provocative. The Pharisee is not a cartoon villain – he was genuinely devout. That is the point. Devout, careful, religiously accomplished – and not justified. The tax collector had none of that, and went home right with God. The logic is inescapable.

Romans 4:4-5 gives Paul’s sharpest summary: there is a difference between a wage and a gift. Salvation is a gift. Abraham, the father of the faithful, was credited with righteousness through faith – not through his subsequent works.

pastorals.body:
The opening ’Common Misunderstanding’ callout on the word ’grace’ is important because the group will define it in various ways – many of them shaped by partial understanding. Listen carefully to how people respond before moving into the teaching content.

The ’Deeper’ question following Ephesians 2 – about Paul including himself in the diagnosis, as a former Pharisee – is worth using if the group has capacity. It makes the point that religious achievement cannot fill the gap between us and God; Paul had more of it than anyone, and still called himself ’dead.’

The parable of the Pharisee and the tax collector will be uncomfortable for some group members. The Pharisee’s prayer sounds like a good prayer – it is grateful, it is honest about his practice. Help the group see what is missing: any sense of need, any acknowledgement of dependence on God’s mercy. Then ask: what would a prayer shaped by grace actually sound like?

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for people who hear ’grace’ and feel relief but then quietly wonder whether it is too easy – whether something is being let off the hook. The session addresses this (v.10 on works), but the instinct runs deep. It is worth naming directly: grace is not permission to coast; it is the only foundation on which genuine obedience becomes possible.

chapters.title: What Does It Mean to Follow Jesus? Discipleship

keyPoints.body:
Having established what the gospel is and how it is received, the series now turns to what it looks like to live as a follower of Jesus. This session is a practical and honest account of discipleship – not the triumphant version, but the real one, which includes cost, community, ongoing dependence, and fruit that comes from connection rather than effort.

Mark 8:34-38 is deliberately confronting. Denying oneself, taking up a cross daily, losing one’s life in order to find it – this is demanding language, and the session does not soften it. For many in this group, the cost of following Jesus is not hypothetical. This passage names that cost and frames it within the larger question of what is truly worth living for.

John 15:1-11 provides the counterbalance: discipleship is not primarily effort – it is remaining. The branch does not strain to produce fruit; it stays connected to the vine and fruit comes naturally from that relationship. This image is pastorally vital for people who have spent their lives trying to earn God’s approval through effort.

The four discipleship rhythms – staying close to Jesus, living in community, obedience as love, and Christlike character – give the group a practical framework. The Likert scale on these rhythms is designed to surface honest self-assessment, not performance.

pastorals.body:
The anticipated leader questions at the end of this session are among the most important in the series. The questions about hiding faith from family, failing and falling back into old patterns, church attendance, and old religious practices are all live pastoral realities for people in this group. Read each response carefully before the session and internalise it – you will need it.

The question ’Can I really follow Jesus if my family doesn’t know yet?’ deserves pastoral wisdom more than a doctrinal answer. Many in the group will be in exactly this situation. Discipleship does not require immediate public declaration in every context. What it requires is an honest orientation of the heart.

The closing text – ‘You are not following a set of rules. You are following a person’ – is a summary of the whole series so far. Let it stand. Don’t add to it.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for people who experience this session as overwhelming – as though discipleship is yet another performance demand they cannot meet. The vine-and-branches image in John 15 is the corrective: the call is to stay connected, not to produce on your own. Return to that image if the weight of the session becomes too heavy.

chapters.title: New Identity, New Family: Belonging in Christ

keyPoints.body:
This is the session that most directly addresses the deepest pastoral reality for many in this group: the question of belonging. Coming to faith in Christ often means losing the community that has defined you – family, neighbourhood, religious identity. This session does not minimise that loss. It honours it, and then answers it.

John 1:12-13 establishes the foundational status: believers are given ’the right to become God’s children.’ This is not earned, not temporary, and not dependent on family of origin. The new birth is ’not of a family line’ – and for people whose identity has been entirely bound up in bloodline and community, this is both disorienting and liberating.

Romans 8:14-17 adds the experiential dimension: the Spirit himself bears witness to our spirit that we are God’s children. The Aramaic word Abba – used by Jesus in Gethsemane at the moment of his deepest anguish – becomes the Spirit-given cry of every believer. This is intimacy at the point of suffering, not only at the point of joy.

Hebrews 10:19-25 and Galatians 3:26-28 complete the picture: there is a community to belong to – one that crosses every ethnic, social, and cultural boundary – and it is constituted not by shared background but by shared standing before God. This is the family the gospel creates.

pastorals.body:
The opening reflection – ‘Since coming to faith, what has it cost you in terms of belonging?’ – is the most personally significant question in the series. It is deliberately private (‘you do not have to share this out loud’), but holding it in mind shapes everything that follows. Be attentive to anyone who appears visibly affected by it.

The section ’A Reflection on What Has Been Lost – and What Has Been Given’ (at the end of the session) is important pastoral territory. Do not skip it or rush it. The session is not trying to replace the grief of real loss with cheap consolation. It is trying to place that grief within a larger truth: the losses are real, and the new family is also real.

The ’Deeper’ question on Galatians 3 – about what makes unity across backgrounds real and not merely theoretical – is a gift if your group is ready for it. The answer involves the ordinary things: gathering, sharing meals, knowing one another’s names. Help the group see that this is not optional idealism; it is the shape of the new community God is building.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for people who hear the language of ’God’s family’ and find it hollow – because their actual experience of church or Christian community has involved rejection, suspicion, or cultural displacement. Validate that experience honestly. The promise in this session is not that every Christian community will feel like home immediately. It is that the belonging is real, and worth pursuing.

chapters.title: Living with Confidence: Witness and Perseverance

keyPoints.body:
This final session brings the series to its close with two interlocking themes: witness and perseverance – and the unshakeable foundation (Romans 8:31-39) on which both rest. It is deliberately forward-looking: the series ends, but the life it has been building toward continues.

Matthew 5:14-16 frames witness as an identity declaration, not an instruction to perform. ’You are the light of the world’ – not ’you should try to become a light.’ The goal of witness is not that people admire the believer, but that they encounter God through them. This reframing releases the performance pressure that makes witness feel impossible.

1 Peter 3:13-16 is the most practical text in the session: be ready to give an account of the hope you possess, with courtesy and respect. The picture is not of a debater winning arguments but of a person living in such a way that others become genuinely curious. The questions come to you. You answer simply, honestly, with respect.

The section on handling pressure and opposition is honest about what many in this group are already experiencing. Peter does not promise suffering will be avoided – he says suffering for doing right is not evidence of God’s absence. It may be the opposite. This is a hard truth, but a necessary one.

Romans 8:31-39 is the final foundation: nothing – not death, not life, not angels, not any power – can separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord. This is Paul’s unshakeable conviction, built on the evidence of the cross. Let it be the last thing the group hears together.

pastorals.body:
The four anticipated questions in the leader notes section are among the most important pastoral conversations you may have: what if family finds out and things get worse; what if I’m not confident enough to speak; what about my Muslim family’s eternal standing; what happens after this series ends. Each deserves a careful, warm, and honest response. Prepare for all four.

The question ’What is the most significant thing that has changed in how you understand God, Jesus, or yourself?’ – the first of the closing personal reflections – is an opportunity to witness growth you may not have noticed session by session. Listen carefully to the answers. Name what you have seen in each person if you have the relationship to do so.

The final session deserves more than a normal ending. The leader notes callout on ’How to End Well’ is not optional sentiment – it is good pastoral practice. Read Romans 8:38-39 aloud together as a declaration. Pray for each person by name. Make sure no one leaves without a clear next step and a known person to contact.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for people who reach the end of the series feeling the weight of what comes next more than the joy of what has happened. The transition out of a structured group can feel destabilising – especially for those who have found this their only safe community. Be concrete about what ongoing support, community, and discipleship looks like beyond Session 10. No one should leave without a next step.

paras: Rick Creighton grew up in Northern Ireland and studied maths at Oxford and then taught maths at a secondary school in England. After that, he studied theology at Moore Theological College in Sydney and London School of Theology.

He served as a church pastor in England for ten years, before moving to Nigeria in 2012, where he has been teaching at ECWA Theological Seminary Kagoro and Jos ECWA Theological Seminary. He has been involved with BeaconLight Training since 2003.
Rick is married to Alanna. They have three children: Asha, Conor, and Jack.

publisher.name: BeaconLight
paras: BeaconLight is a non-denominational Christian training organisation based in the UK, founded in 1995. Its aim is to equip Christians worldwide with practically-applied Biblical gospel ministry tools – resources designed to help people both equip themselves and equip others.

BeaconLight’s resources include CrossCheck, which enables Christians to explain their faith and lead others to Jesus, and Word@Work, a verse-by-verse applied devotional commentary of the whole New Testament. BeaconLight also provides printed Biblical resources to prisons, seafarers, police, and the armed services.
BeaconLight believes that Christian ministry should be free of charge, and all its online materials are available without registration or payment. The ministry is funded entirely by donations.
BeaconLight is a member of the Evangelical Alliance and Global Connections, and is a registered charity in England and Wales (Charity No. 1047046).
